text: This video will highlight the steps in a hellermyotomy, which is the most effective treatment for a condition called achalasia. There are typically five ports placed, one for the camera, one for the liver retractor, and three working ports.
The surgeon lifts the liver off the stomach to expose the junction of the esophagus and stomach. Connective tissue overlying this region is carefully divided, avoiding damage to adjacent structures.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector to perform tissue dissection, utilizing vibrations to cut and seal vessels while minimizing thermal damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses gentle blunt dissection to enter the chest and expose the esophagus, dividing loose tissues with a sweeping motion. When encountering a vessel, it is sealed with a coagulator. The instrument's hot tip is cooled on the liver to prevent injury to adjacent organs.
The surgeon divides the fat pad overlying the gastroesophageal junction, carefully raising the vagus nerve off the esophagus to prevent injury during the myotomy.

An eight-centimeter umbilical tape is introduced into the surgical field to measure the length of the myotomy, which involves dividing the sphincter muscle. The myotomy extends six centimeters into the esophagus and two centimeters into the stomach.
The surgeon marks the stomach with electrocautery. After marking, the umbilical tape is removed, and a dilator is inserted into the esophagus to assist with the myotomy procedure.

The surgeon begins the myotomy on the anterior border of the esophagus, using an ultrasonic dissector. The cool white end of the device slides under the muscle layers, lifting them off the inner mucosal layer to prevent injury, while effectively dividing the muscle and limiting bleeding.

The surgeon continues the division of the sphincter muscle well into the chest, ensuring complete separation of all muscle layers at least six centimeters proximal to the gastroesophageal junction, to alleviate the obstruction to food passage.

The surgeon carries the myotomy down into the anterior wall of the stomach, carefully dissecting through the three muscle layers: longitudinal, circular, and diagonal. Using electrocautery, the deepest diagonal muscle fibers are lifted off the mucosa and divided, with slow and deliberate progress to prevent mucosal injury.

The surgeon inspects the mucosa after completing the myotomy, carefully dividing any residual muscle fibers. Sutures are placed in areas with potential injury or risk of bleeding to prevent postoperative complications.
This requires that your surgeon be comfortable with laparoscopic sewing, as the mucosa is quite fragile and further injury can occur if not performed properly.

A video endoscope is inserted into the esophagus, emitting light to assess for mucosal leaks and ensure complete division of muscle fibers. The endoscope helps confirm that the myotomy extends sufficiently into the stomach, with internal markings aiding in identifying the end of the esophagus.

The surgeon performs the final phase of the cardiomytomy by creating an anterior wrap to prevent postoperative reflux. The lateral attachments and blood vessels are divided to free the stomach, enhancing its mobility and reducing tension on the wrap. The dissection is extended up to the diaphragm, positioned to the left of the esophagus.
The surgeon places sutures from the stomach through the cut edge of the muscle and secures them to the diaphragm, ensuring the muscle edges are properly tacked.
The surgeon creates an anterior wrap over the exposed mucosa to seal it, marsupialize the edges of the cut muscle to prevent scarring, and reduce postoperative reflux or indigestion.

The surgeon places three separate sutures to attach the anterior wrap to the left diaphragm. Following this, three additional sutures are placed to secure the wrap to the right diaphragm, completing the wrap and covering the esophageal mucosa.

The surgeon places and ties the final sutures, completing the anterior wrap where the stomach is positioned in front of the esophagus and myotomy, and attached to the diaphragm. This configuration visually forms a one-way valve intended to reduce acid reflux into the esophagus.
